edges.description: Failed sucrose and starch digestion leads to intestinal malabsorption and osmotic symptoms after carbohydrate exposure.
Loss of brush-border SI directly reduces sucrase activity.
SI deficiency variably reduces isomaltase activity, ranging from absent to almost normal.
Misfolding and trafficking defects reduce apical SI availability at the enterocyte brush border.
edges.source: Abnormal SI folding and apical trafficking

edges.description: Unabsorbed carbohydrate in the intestinal lumen drives osmotic diarrhea after sucrose or starch exposure.
Fermentation and malabsorption of undigested carbohydrate produce bloating and abdominal distention.
Abdominal pain is a common postprandial symptom in SI variant carriers and CSID families.
intermediate_mechanisms: Carbohydrate malabsorption causes gas production, intestinal distention, and postprandial symptoms.

edges.description: Orphanet records vomiting as a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
Orphanet records nausea as a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
Orphanet records constipation as a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation in congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
Orphanet records gastroesophageal reflux as a frequent manifestation of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
Orphanet records poor appetite as a frequent manifestation of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
Orphanet records flatulence as a frequent gastrointestinal manifestation of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
Chronic carbohydrate malabsorption can lead to poor growth and failure to thrive.
intermediate_mechanisms: Chronic diarrhea and nutrient malabsorption impair growth.

edges.description: Persistent carbohydrate malabsorption and dietary intolerance can cause malnutrition.
intermediate_mechanisms: Chronic dietary nutrient malabsorption limits effective nutrient uptake.

edges.description: Sucrose malabsorption can be detected noninvasively by sucrose hydrogen breath testing.
intermediate_mechanisms: Undigested sucrose reaches intestinal microbial metabolism.
Breath hydrogen increases after sucrose challenge when sucrose is malabsorbed.

edges.description: Reduced sucrase activity directly measures loss of brush-border SI catalytic function.
Reduced or absent isomaltase activity is a variable biochemical readout of SI deficiency and helps explain variable starch intolerance.
Increased breath hydrogen after sucrose challenge reports sucrose malabsorption downstream of SI deficiency.

edges.target: Abnormal SI folding and apical trafficking

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency: Same Mutation with Different Clinical Presentations.
evidence.snippet: abdominal pain (37%)

nodes.description: A subset of SI variants causes abnormal protein folding, increased turnover, endoplasmic reticulum retention, and impaired apical sorting, further reducing effective brush-border enzyme activity.
nodes.id: Abnormal SI folding and apical trafficking

evidence.reference_title: The patient journey to diagnosis and treatment of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the SI gene result in abnormal synthesis and/or incorrect transport of the SI enzyme.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular basis of aberrant apical protein transport in an intestinal enzyme disorder.
evidence.snippet: The impaired sorting profile to the apical membrane of human intestinal sucrase-isomaltase is the underlying cause in the pathogenesis of a novel phenotype of intestinal congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Altered folding, turnover, and polarized sorting act in concert to define a novel pathomechanism of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Altogether, the combined effects of the C635R mutation on the turnover rate, function, polarized sorting, and detergent solubility of SI constitute a unique and novel pathomechanism of CSID.

evidence.reference_title: Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency Causing Persistent Bloating and Diarrhea in an Adult Female.
evidence.snippet: We present a case of a 50-year-old woman with persistent symptoms of bloating in spite of extensive evaluation and treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency: Same Mutation with Different Clinical Presentations.
evidence.snippet: bloating (22.2%)

nodes.description: Enterocyte brush-border sucrase-isomaltase deficiency reduces hydrolysis of dietary sucrose and often impairs digestion of starch-derived carbohydrates, producing primary carbohydrate maldigestion.

evidence.reference_title: The patient journey to diagnosis and treatment of congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (CSID) is a rare genetic disorder characterized by a deficiency of the sucrase-isomaltase (SI) enzyme complex within the brush border membrane of the small intestine.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and acquired sucrase-isomaltase deficiency: A clinical review.
evidence.snippet: Genetic sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (GSID) is an inherited deficiency in the ability to digest sucrose and potentially starch due to mutations in the sucrase-isomaltase (SI) gene.

nodes.description: Undigested sucrose and starch-derived carbohydrates remain in the intestinal lumen, producing chronic intestinal malabsorption with osmotic diarrhea and associated nutritional consequences.

evidence.reference_title: Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency Causing Persistent Bloating and Diarrhea in an Adult Female.
evidence.snippet: Congenital sucrase isomaltase deficiency (CSID) is an autosomal recessive disorder which leads to chronic intestinal malabsorption of nutrients from ingested starch and sucrose.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency: Same Mutation with Different Clinical Presentations.
evidence.snippet: Congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency is an autosomal recessive inherited disaccharidase deficiency characterized by chronic osmotic diarrhea.

evidence.snippet: Mutations in the SI gene result in abnormal synthesis and/or incorrect transport of the SI enzyme.

nodes.description: Oral sacrosidase can supplement deficient sucrase activity, although some patients still need ongoing starch restriction.

evidence.snippet: Sacrosidase | Accepted_Therapeutic_Use_For | - | - | congenital sucrase-isomaltase deficiency (CSID)

evidence.reference_title: Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency Causing Persistent Bloating and Diarrhea in an Adult Female.
evidence.snippet: Treatment of CSID consists of limiting sucrose in diet and replacement therapy with sacrosidase.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and acquired sucrase-isomaltase deficiency: A clinical review.
evidence.snippet: As this approach may be challenging, additional treatment with commercially available sacrosidase is available. However, some patients may require continued starch restriction.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency: Same Mutation with Different Clinical Presentations.
evidence.snippet: Sacrosidase enzyme replacement was applied to 7 patients whose symptoms did not improve with dietary elimination. Clinical response was obtained after enzyme treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Sucrase-Isomaltase Deficiency Causing Persistent Bloating and Diarrhea in an Adult Female.
evidence.snippet: Diagnosis is suspected on detailed patient history and confirmed by a disaccharidase assay using small intestinal biopsies or sucrose hydrogen breath test.

readouts.interpretation: Increased breath hydrogen after sucrose challenge reports sucrose malabsorption downstream of SI deficiency.

nodes.description: Dietary restriction of sucrose and, when needed, starch is first-line management and should be individualized to symptom tolerance.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and acquired sucrase-isomaltase deficiency: A clinical review.
evidence.snippet: Management of GSID is based on sucrose and potentially starch restriction tailored to the individual patients' tolerance and symptoms.
